Article.ArticleTitle: Randomised controlled effectiveness study (RCT) of isometric exercise (IE) in adults with stage 1 and 2 hypertension - ISOFITTER study.

Abstract.AbstractText: High blood pressure (BP) affects more than one in four adults in England and only one in three patients are being treated effectively. Treatment of high BP includes changes to lifestyle such as more physical activity and/or taking medication. However, low adoption and high attrition rates are common with current large targets for recommended exercise (>150 minutes moderate exercise per week plus 2 strength sessions). Evidence suggests that isometric exercise (IE), holding a fixed body position for a period of time, for example a wall squat, lowers BP a greater amount, with less time and effort, than other recommended exercise. This ISOFITTER study will provide robust effectiveness evidence of IE for hypertension.

A multi-centre, randomised, controlled trial of isometric exercise wall squat intervention for hypertension: an effectiveness-implementation hybrid type-1 design. Adults (n=542) with Stage 1 or Stage 2 hypertension, on no more than one antihypertensive, and no other medical contra-indications will be randomised to either a standard care plus IE intervention group or standard care control group. Blood pressure readings, fidelity measurements, medications, adverse events, quality of life, participant satisfaction and health service use will be collected at baseline, week 4, month 3 and month 6 with a subgroup of n=50 invited up to month 12. Qualitative participant focus groups and interviews with wider stakeholders will collect implementation data.

The ISOFITTER study will establish effectiveness of a self-administered, home IE intervention in lowering blood pressure in people with uncomplicated stage 1 and 2 hypertension. Implementation evidence will support patient delivery, context for scaling up of the intervention and intervention cost.

Lifestyle changes for the treatment of hypertension in the absence of other risk factors should not be overlooked. For long term hypertension management, easily adopted, evidenced exercise interventions are needed. This study will help to address this evidence gap.

OtherAbstract.AbstractText: This research aims to see if a wall-squat isometric exercise training programme (leaning with your back against the wall in a sitting position) will reduce peoples blood pressure for those diagnosed with high blood pressure.

High blood pressure affects more than one in four adults in England and only one in three of these successfully reduce their blood pressure through treatment. The cost of this to the NHS is high (approximately £2.1 billion per year), mainly due to care related to strokes and heart attacks that can be caused by high blood pressure. Treatment of high blood pressure includes changes to lifestyle such as healthy eating and exercise and/or taking medication. A type of exercise, called isometric exercise, involves holding a fixed body position for a set amount of time. This type of exercise includes things like leaning with your back against the wall in a seated position and has been shown to be better at lowering blood pressure than other types of exercise like running. This study follows on from smaller research projects to see if a wall-squat isometric exercise is good for lowering blood pressure as it was found to be easily done at home to help lower blood pressure. Healthcare professionals also thought it was a good exercise option for those with high blood pressure. This larger study is now needed to be confident that this isometric exercise can reduce people's blood pressure reliably, at home.

This study has been designed with public advisors and will involve 542 adults with a high blood pressure diagnosis, without any other significant health conditions. We will use a wide approach to asking people to take part, including those from less represented backgrounds. The study will be advertised through social media, community groups, health services and GP surgeries. All 542 people that take part will receive healthy lifestyle advice. Half of those taking part will then be randomly chosen to do the isometric exercise and the other half with be a control group and asked not to change anything. This control group will be offered the exercise after 6 months. The isometric exercise plan involves completing three wall-squat sessions per week for six months. All those taking part will record their blood pressure and other lifestyle information at four-weeks, three-months and six-months so we can see whether the exercise has made a difference.
